title: Almond biscotti
subtitle: Twice-baked Italian almond biscuits
excerpt: Traditional Italian biscotti — hard, dry, twice-baked biscuits studded with whole almonds. The first bake sets the log; the second crisps the slices to the characteristic snap. Eaten with coffee or a glass of vin santo.
sourceNotes: Cantucci are documented in Tuscan domestic cookery from the seventeenth century. The name biscotti derives from the Latin bis coctus — twice cooked. The recipe appears consistently as: plain flour, sugar, eggs, almonds, baking powder; no butter or fat; baked as a log, sliced, and rebaked. This version follows the standard Tuscan formula.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze in a sealed box. Biscotti keep up to 6 months frozen.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Keeps for 3 weeks in a sealed tin. Improves with time as they dry further.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: fan oven, middle shelf — 170°C for the first bake, 140°C for the second

glossaryTerms.definition: Biscotti are baked in two stages: first as a whole log until set and lightly coloured, then sliced and baked again at a lower temperature to dry out and crisp the cut surfaces. The second bake removes almost all remaining moisture, giving biscotti their characteristic hardness and their shelf life of several weeks.

content.text: Biscotti are one of the few biscuits with no butter in the dough. Fat would make them tender; the whole point of a biscotto is that it is hard.
drives out the moisture that eggs bring in, and what remains is a crisp, dry biscuit that softens gradually when you dip it into coffee.
Heat the oven to 170°C fan. Line a baking tray with baking paper.

Mix the {{plain-flour}}, {{caster-sugar}}, {{baking-powder}}, and {{fine-sea-salt}} in a large bowl. Beat in the {{free-range-eggs}} and {{vanilla-extract}} until a stiff, sticky dough forms. Mix in the {{whole-almonds}}.

With floured hands, shape the dough into two logs about 25 cm long and 6 cm wide on the lined tray. Bake at 170°C fan for 25–28 minutes until set and pale gold. Remove from the oven and leave to cool for 15 minutes. Reduce the oven to 140°C fan.

Cut each log on the diagonal into slices about 1 cm thick. Lay the slices flat on the tray and bake at 140°C fan for 15–18 minutes until dry and lightly golden on the cut surfaces. Cool completely on a rack. They crisp further as they cool.
